message.abstract:
<jats:title>Summary</jats:title>
                <jats:p>During 2021, COVID-19 vaccinations were delivered much more rapidly in some countries than in others. Ethical principles would have suggested allocating available vaccines to people by age, irrespective of where they live, because mortality risks from COVID-19 are much higher for older people. The World Health Organization recommended initial allocations of vaccines to countries based on their total population size, in part due to uncertainty about how COVID-19 would affect different countries.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>This paper estimates how many people would have died from COVID-19 up to 31 October 2021 if either of these allocation rules had been applied, compared to estimates of actual COVID-19 deaths. The estimates suggest that allocating vaccines by age would have resulted in between 500,000 and 1,500,000 fewer deaths globally (with a best estimate of 1,090,000 fewer deaths), while allocating vaccines between countries based on national population sizes would have reduced total deaths globally by between 450,000 and 2,100,000 (with a best estimate of 1,440,000 fewer deaths).</jats:p>
                <jats:p>Most low-and middle-income countries would have seen reductions in deaths, with the greatest absolute numbers in large middle-income countries (especially Bangladesh, India and Indonesia). More deaths would have taken place in many high-income countries, with the greatest absolute numbers in the United States and Turkey, and the greatest percentage changes in Arabian Peninsula countries, Israel and some island states. In most European Union countries, deaths would not have differed much if vaccines were allocated by age, because they would have received more vaccine doses during the early months of 2021 but fewer later in the year.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>Although allocation of vaccines by age should intuitively lead to fewest deaths, the estimated deaths would have been even lower if vaccines were allocated based on population size. Allocation by population would have directed disproportionate numbers of vaccines to a set of countries – especially India, Bangladesh and Indonesia – which experienced large outbreaks due to the Delta variant in 2021 after having previously limited infections through “ flattening the curve”.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>Sequencing of vaccination by age in national vaccination rollouts is critical to maximizing the numbers of lives saved. The estimated gains from fairer global vaccination allocation would be greater if high-income and upper-middle-income countries did not sequence vaccinations by age cohort, and would be lower if lower-middle-income and low-income countries did not vaccinate most of their elderly before the general population, whether due to policy choices or people not accepting vaccines made available to them or logistical difficulties in vaccine delivery.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>The estimates correspond to a reduction of between 8.5% and 10.7% (with a best estimate of 10.4%) of the total estimated actual deaths from COVID-19 between 1 January and 31 October 2021, if vaccines were allocated by age, and between 7.8% and 15.0% (with a best estimate of 13.6%) of total estimated actual deaths, if vaccines were allocated based on national population sizes. These percentages are small, despite the large differences in vaccine deployment between countries, because the mostly high-income countries which vaccinated their populations faster have disproportionately large numbers of elderly people. If a future SARS-CoV-2 variant, or a future pandemic, were to have fatality rates that are similar across age groups, or that are higher for children and young adults, then unequal global allocation of vaccines would have a much more severe effect on overall global mortality than it has for COVID-19 so far.</jats:p>
title: Estimates of the impact on COVID-19 deaths of unequal global allocations of vaccines

reference.article-title: A global database of COVID-19 vaccinations

reference.unstructured: Reuters. COVID-19 Vaccination Tracker. https://graphics.reuters.com/world-coronavirus-tracker-and-maps/vaccination-rollout-and-access/. Most countries planned first to vaccinate frontline health care workers and sometimes other essential workers (with lists of professions that vary by country). Most countries planned to vaccinate the elderly before the general population; plans in higher-income countries typically sequence vaccinations by age cohorts, while plans in lower-income countries tend to include one or two groups of older people. Many countries have provisions to vaccinate people with medical conditions that put them at increased risk from COVID-19 prior to the general population.

World Health Organization Strategic Advisory Group of Experts on Immunization (SAGE) Working Group on COVID-19 Vaccines. WHO SAGE Roadmap for Prioritizing Uses of COVID-19 Vaccines in the Context of Limited Supply. WHO (13 November 2020). https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/who-sage-roadmap-for-prioritizing-uses-of-covid-19-vaccines-in-the-context-of-limited-supply.

reference.article-title: An ethical framework for global vaccines allocation

reference.unstructured: World Health Organization. Fair allocation mechanism for COVID-19 vaccines through the COVAX Facility. WHO (9 September 2020). https://www.who.int/publications/m/item/fair-allocation-mechanism-for-covid-19-vaccines-through-the-covax-facility.

The calculations include 183 countries and territories, with 98% of the world’s population. Excluded are 52 countries and territories, which are not covered by the Our World In Data datasets, or for which the Our World In Data does not cover the full period from 1 January 2021 to 31 October 2021, or for which there are 5 or fewer data points on numbers of vaccine doses delivered (equivalent to less than one data point per month between April and September 2021). The largest countries, by population, excluded from the calculations are Tanzania, Algeria, Democratic People’s Republic of Korea, Burundi, Turkmenistan, Eritrea, Puerto Rico and Kosovo, and most of the remainder are small island states and territories.
World Health Organization. Coronavirus Disease (COVID-19) Dashboard. WHO (2020). https://covid19.who.int.

The Economist. The pandemic’s true death toll: our daily estimate of excess deaths around the world. The Economist (2021). https://www.economist.com/graphic-detail/coronavirus-excess-deaths-estimates. Accessed on 15 November 2021. The calculations use alternatives to the death detection ratios (DDRs) derived from The Economist model’s numbers for a few countries, as follow:
United Nations, Department of Economic and Social Affairs, Population Division. World Population Prospects 2019, Online Edition. Rev. 1.

reference.article-title: Age-specific mortality and immunity patterns of SARS-CoV-2

reference.unstructured: We assume that mortality risk is reduced by 90% for people attacked by the SARS-CoV- 2 virus, due either to avoiding infection entirely or to reduced severity of disease if infected. Alternative assumptions are considered in the sensitivity analyses. Most of the commonly used vaccines are reported to reduce mortality by 90% of more, for all of the SARS-CoV-2 variants prior to Omicron. Almost all of the commonly used vaccines appear to reduce the probability of severe disease by 70% or more – according to the vaccine efficacy tracker maintained by the Institute for Health Metrics and Evaluation at https://www.healthdata.org/covid/covid-19-vaccine-efficacy-summary. Furthermore, the error margins for the reduction of mortality for different vaccines overlap, so it is possible to say definitively that one is more effective than another at preventing death. Vaccination effectiveness is roughly consistent across age cohorts up to 90, but decreases for people older due to immunosenescence; our modelling assumes constant reduction in mortality across all ages for simplicity. References to various studies of vaccine effectiveness can be found in the following Wikipedia entries:
Covaxin: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Covaxin#Efficacy
Janssen (Johnson & Johnson): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Janssen_COVID-19_vaccine#Efficacy
Moderna: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moderna_COVID-19_vaccine#Effectiveness
Novovax: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Novavax_COVID-19_vaccine#Efficacy
Oxford-AstraZeneca: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxford%E2%80%93AstraZeneca_COVID-19_vaccine#Effectiveness
Pfizer-BioNTech: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pfizer%E2%80%93BioNTech_COVID-19_vaccine#Effectiveness
Sinopharm BIBP: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sinopharm_BIBP_COVID-19_vaccine#Effectiveness
Sinovac CoronaVac: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CoronaVac#Effectiveness
Sputnik V : https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sputnik_V_COVID-19_vaccine#Effectiveness

See, for example, https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/vaccines/keythingstoknow.html and https://www.health.gov.au/initiatives-and-programs/covid-19-vaccines/is-it-true/is-it-true-how-long-does-it-take-to-have-immunity-after-vaccination.

According to the World Health Organization, average times between infection and onset of symptoms about 5 to 6 days (see https://www.who.int/emergencies/diseases/novel-coronavirus-2019/question-and-answers-hub/q-a-detail/coronavirus-disease-covid-19). The UK Office of National Statistics quotes two studies which find median times between onset of symptoms and death of 16 and 19 days (see https://www.ons.gov.uk/peoplepopulationandcommunity/healthandsocialcare/conditionsanddiseases/articles/coronaviruscovid19infectionsurveytechnicalarticle/wavesandlagsofcovid19inenglandjune2021).
J.P. Callan , C.J.A. Nouwen , A.S. Lexmond , and O. Fourtassi . Categorizing the status of COVID-19 outbreaks around the world. https://doi.org/10.1101/2021.03.08.21252586.

S.V. Subramanian and A. Kumar . Increases in COVID-19 are unrelated to levels of vaccination across 68 countries and 2947 counties in the United States. European Journal of Epidemiology (2021). https://doi.org/10.1007/s10654-021-00808-7.

N.F. Brazeau , R. Verity , S. Jenks , H. Fu , C. Whittaker , P. Winskill , et al. COVID-19 Infection Fatality Ratio: Estimates from Seroprevalence. (Imperial College London 2020). https://doi.org/10.25561/83545.
